case_name: Cobalt and manganese control from reservoir water for groundwater recharge under seasonal and energy-cost constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for cobalt- and manganese-impacted reservoir water intended for groundwater recharge while balancing seasonal raw-water variability, cost, and energy constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A regional utility wants to divert reservoir water to managed aquifer recharge basins during dry months, but late-summer stratification and periodic runoff events increase dissolved metals and suspended solids. The recharge permit is focused on protecting aquifer quality, so cobalt and manganese must be reduced reliably while avoiding high-energy treatment that would make seasonal operation uneconomic. Several technically feasible routes exist, but the preferred option must balance metal removal performance with robustness to seasonal swings in turbidity and organic matter, manageable sludge production, and modest operator burden.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should be suitable for groundwater recharge with stable reduction of cobalt and manganese to levels protective of receiving aquifer quality and infiltration operations.
secondary_goals: maintain reliable treatment during seasonal turbidity and DOC changes
limit chemical and sludge handling burden
preserve a practical seasonal operating strategy

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer conventional sludge streams over difficult brines or regenerant wastes
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: process must tolerate seasonal increases in turbidity, DOC, and metal speciation shifts without frequent process upsets

core_unit_functions.pre_oxidation_with_permanganate_or_aerat: promote oxidation of dissolved manganese and facilitate conversion of cobalt-associated species to particulate forms that can be removed downstream
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: capture oxidized metal solids and reservoir colloids including NOM-associated metal fractions
core_unit_functions.sedimentation_or_high_rate_clarification: remove bulk floc and reduce solids loading to filters
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish remaining suspended solids and metal particulates before recharge

applicability_rationale: this route balances metal removal, seasonal solids handling, and relatively low energy demand better than pressure-driven membranes
reservoir water with moderate DOC and variable turbidity generally benefits from clarification ahead of filtration
manganese can often be improved substantially by controlled oxidation and filtration, while cobalt can be reduced through oxidation plus coagulation capture of particulate or adsorbed fractions
the process uses familiar unit operations with manageable operator burden for seasonal service
conventional sludge handling is typically easier to manage than membrane concentrate or ion-exchange regenerant waste
potential_risks: dissolved cobalt removal may be incomplete if a large fraction remains truly soluble after oxidation and coagulation
oxidant dose control is important because underdosing can leave manganese breakthrough and overdosing can affect downstream solids characteristics
seasonal cold-water periods may slow oxidation kinetics and require closer monitoring

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: reduce turbidity and NOM that interfere with catalytic media performance
core_unit_functions.clarification: remove floc and stabilize solids loading
core_unit_functions.manganese_dioxide_coated_media_filtratio: provide catalytic oxidation and filtration for manganese and some cobalt-bearing particulates

applicability_rationale: catalytic media can be effective for manganese control with moderate energy demand and compact footprint
placing clarification ahead of the media improves resilience during reservoir turbidity spikes
this route can outperform plain dual-media filtration for manganese when influent chemistry is well controlled
it remains less energy intensive than nanofiltration or reverse osmosis
potential_risks: cobalt removal may be less predictable than manganese removal and may depend on oxidation state and particulate association
media performance can degrade if pH or oxidant residual are not well maintained
seasonal DOC and algae-derived foulants may shorten run lengths or increase maintenance

core_unit_functions.pH_adjustment: increase pH to favor metal hydroxide precipitation
core_unit_functions.chemical_precipitation_with_hydroxide: convert dissolved cobalt and some other metals into settleable precipitates
core_unit_functions.sand_or_dual_media_filtration: polish residual metal particulates before recharge

applicability_rationale: hydroxide precipitation is a technically plausible route for cobalt reduction and uses conventional equipment
it can be attractive when dissolved cobalt removal rather than only particulate capture is the main compliance concern
the chemistry is generally understandable and compatible with standard solids separation processes
potential_risks: cobalt precipitation may require elevated pH, increasing chemical consumption and the need for post-adjustment before recharge
manganese precipitation is less favorable at moderate pH unless oxidation is also well managed
sludge production can be significant and more chemically dense than simple coagulation sludge
seasonal raw-water changes can make pH control and settleability less stable

core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration_pretreatment: remove suspended solids and reduce membrane fouling load
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration: reject dissolved multivalent metals and associated complexes to achieve strong finished water quality

applicability_rationale: nanofiltration is technically capable of strong cobalt and manganese reduction and provides a robust barrier for dissolved species
this option may be attractive if future recharge requirements tighten for a wider set of dissolved contaminants
the compact footprint can help where site area is constrained
potential_risks: high specific energy demand conflicts with the seasonal and cost-sensitive operating model
concentrate management is difficult for recharge projects and may require off-site disposal or a separate waste strategy
membrane fouling risk increases during seasonal DOC and algae events
operator skill and cleaning requirements are materially higher than for conventional treatment

core_unit_functions.media_filtration: reduce suspended solids to protect downstream resin
core_unit_functions.chelating_ion_exchange: selectively remove dissolved cobalt and some other divalent metals

applicability_rationale: ion exchange is technically capable for dissolved cobalt under controlled water chemistry
it can achieve low residual metal concentrations when competing ions and foulants are limited
included here as a plausible but less aligned route under the stated reservoir-water conditions
potential_risks: moderate DOC, seasonal solids, and variable reservoir organics increase fouling risk and reduce resin practicality
competing divalent ions and manganese can reduce cobalt selectivity and increase regeneration frequency
regenerant waste handling is less desirable than conventional sludge for this project
the process is less robust to raw-water swings than clarification-plus-filtration systems

preferred_rank_pattern: conventional oxidation_plus_clarification_plus_filtration should rank near the top for seasonal reservoir water
catalytic manganese media should appear as a strong alternative but not necessarily first because cobalt control may be less certain
membrane treatment should not rank first under high energy sensitivity and difficult concentrate handling
at least one technically capable dissolved-metal option should rank lower due to mismatch with seasonal fouling or waste constraints

must_reflect: seasonal reservoir variability makes pretreatment and robust solids handling important
energy-sensitive seasonal operation disfavors pressure membrane systems
cost limits favor conventional treatment trains over highly specialized or waste-intensive systems

must_contain: why manganese oxidation and filtration are relevant for this source water
why cobalt may be harder to remove consistently than manganese depending on dissolved versus particulate speciation
why clarification is valuable under turbidity and DOC fluctuations
why concentrate or regenerant waste pushes membranes and ion exchange lower in rank

should_mention: oxidant dose control and breakthrough risk
fouling sensitivity during high DOC or algae periods
